Notes to the Prologue

The Prologue introduces several foundational elements of the origin story, including Shi Zhengli’s early work on SARS viruses, the first detection of the novel virus, and the initial responses from Chinese authorities and the international scientific community.

Notes on Shi Zhengli’s account

Later reporting has raised significant questions about Shi Zhengli’s timeline and recollection of events. Among other things, a laboratory under the Chinese Academy of Medical Sciences in Beijing had already sequenced the full genome of SARS-CoV-2 and shared it with the United States two days before the phone call Shi described (see Chapter 19 and Sources: Strobel 2024 and NCBI GenBank / HHS 2024). Additionally, during the night of 30 December 2019, while Shi was traveling back to Wuhan, the Wuhan Institute of Virology’s virus databases were altered and its online database disappeared — an event that had begun earlier that autumn (see Chapter 10 and Sources: Devine 2020 and Small et al. 2021). For further inconsistencies in Shi Zhengli’s publications regarding the genome sequencing of SARS-CoV-2, see Chapter 11.

Shi’s interview (Qiu 2020) took place while Chinese authorities were already actively suppressing discussion of the outbreak’s origin (Birrell 2020).

Further Reading

Demaneuf, Gilles, BSL-4 laboratories in China: Kunming, Wuhan, Harbin, Medium, April 27, 2022.

Cross-References

  • For the studies discovering the origin of SARS-CoV-1, see Chapter 12 Sources: Li et al. 2005 and Hu et al. 2017.
  • For a detailed discussion of the bat-distance problem, see Chapters 5 and 11.
  • For summaries of Shi Zhengli’s published research, see Chapter 12 Sources and Notes.
  • For more on the Wuhan Institute of Virology’s virus collection programs, see Chapters 11 and 19.
  • For additional acknowledgments, see Chapter 9.
